title: Interview With ‘O.J.: Made in America’ Director Ezra Edelman | The Big Picture (Ep. 3)
format: Sean Fennessey talks with Oscar-nominated director Ezra Edelman about the seven-and-a-half-hour first cut of O.J.: Made in America, reading his way into a subject for months, and what O.J. Simpson explains about race, celebrity, and America.

films.title: O.J.: Made in America
films.note: Commissioned at five hours, screened for ESPN executives at over seven and a half. Edelman says he never cut for time, only for content: the length was the appeal, with O.J. as the lens for a story about Los Angeles, policing, and race.

films.title: Magic & Bird: A Courtship of Rivals
films.note: Cited by Fennessey among Edelman's earlier work; Edelman describes building it as concurrent narratives that cut to black between chapters, the same pacing he brought to O.J.

films.title: Brooklyn Dodgers: The Ghosts of Flatbush
films.note: The two-hour Dodgers film he co-directed at HBO. As a Brooklyn baseball guy himself, he says this is where he found his footing, sinking into a subject over nine or ten months.

films.title: The Curious Case of Curt Flood
films.note: Fennessey singles it out as a favorite; another of Edelman's subjects used prismatically.

films.title: The Naked Gun
films.note: Edelman's shorthand for the affection that made audiences refuse O.J.'s guilt: 'I watched him in the naked gun, and he made me laugh.'
